rounds.position_summary: More likely than not TRUE: on these facts, Okafor likely holds a § 1090 "financial interest" in the Harbor Light contract (through her spouse's management-level, substantially city-funded salary, and/or her own board seat), and because she sits on the very body awarding the contract, well-settled case law holds that her recusal does not cure the defect unless a specific § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies and is properly invoked. Given the seniority of the spouse's role and the contractor's heavy dependence on city funding, this interest looks too substantial to fit comfortably within the narrow, disclosure-based exceptions, so the contract would likely be void under § 1092 despite full recusal — though the exact statutory fit is genuinely uncertain enough to keep this below near-certainty.
rationales: Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, recusal/abstention by an interested member of the very body making the contract does not cure a § 1090 violation, because the statute's prohibition runs to the body's composition, not merely to who casts the vote, absent a fitting statutory exception.

A spouse's salaried, program-director-level (i.e., management/policy-adjacent) position at a nonprofit whose operating budget is ~40% city-contract-funded, and which wins ~60% of the relevant city contracts, is the kind of non-speculative, substantial interest that § 1090's broad case-law construction (e.g., People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289) tends to treat as disqualifying rather than remote.

Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently be shielded by the § 1091.5(a)(8) noninterest exception for a noncompensated nonprofit officer, but that carve-out does not obviously extend to the separate and more consequential interest created by her spouse's compensated employment.

Because § 1090 voids the contract itself under § 1092 once an unexempted interested member sits on the awarding body, the remedy is structural rather than procedural, so 'full recusal' as framed in the proposition is legally insufficient by design if no exception applies (see also Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050 on the breadth of disqualifying interests and limits of curing by non-participation).

The main source of doubt is whether a specific § 1091(b) 'remote interest' category for spousal employment (if one squarely exists and is properly invoked with disclosure) could apply to a Director-of-Programs-level position, which — if it does — would flip the analysis and make the proposition false; I am not fully certain of that subsection's precise text and fit to these facts.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the spouse's Director-of-Programs position and Harbor Light's funding structure fall within (or squarely outside) any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception — that single statutory-fit question is outcome-determinative, and I lack full confidence in the precise current text of § 1091(b)'s spousal-employment-related subsections and how a court would apply them to a senior, policy-adjacent role rather than a rank-and-file job.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not true that the contract would be void or voidable under section 1090 despite full recusal.
rationales: Section 1090 imputes a disqualifying interest to the whole contracting body, so recusal from the vote alone does not free the body to contract, per Thomson v Call and Stigall v City of Taft.
A spouse salary from the contracting nonprofit is likely an imputable financial interest under section 1090, similar to the imputation theory in People v Honig.

Okafor own uncompensated board seat likely qualifies as a remote interest under section 1091, curable by disclosure and abstention, but that exception does not cover the separate spousal salary interest.
Because the spousal salary interest fits no listed remote interest or noninterest exception, it fully disqualifies the council from contracting with Harbor Light regardless of recusal.
The chief counterargument is that a fixed noncontingent salary might be judged too attenuated from this particular contract to count as a disqualifying financial interest at all.
authorities: Stigall v City of Taft 1962 58 Cal 2d 565
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the spouse fixed salaried position, as opposed to contract contingent compensation, would actually be found to satisfy the financial interest threshold under section 1090.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not TRUE. Given the specific facts—spouse's salary materially funded (~40%) by city contract dollars, Okafor remaining a sitting council member throughout the entire framework-and-award process, and no clearly applicable remote-interest or noninterest exception covering the spousal-salary component—California's rule that a board member's recusal does not cure section 1090 (as opposed to Political Reform Act recusal, which generally does cure) should render the Harbor Light award void or voidable even with Okafor fully recused from both votes. The main path to a contrary result would be if the board-seat interest is separately neutralized by a section 1091.5-type nonprofit-officer noninterest provision AND a court finds the spousal-salary nexus too indirect/immaterial to independently count as a disqualifying financial interest—both would have to hold, which I view as less likely than not on these facts.
rationales: Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, section 1090 disables the contracting body itself once any member holds a disqualifying, non-exempt financial interest; the interested member's abstention/recusal from the vote does not cure the defect the way recusal cures a section 87100 problem, so Okafor's continued council membership through the entire RFP-framework and award process is itself likely fatal regardless of how cleanly she recuses.

Okafor almost certainly holds a 'financial interest' in the Harbor Light contract via her community-property stake in her spouse's approximately $95,000 salary, which is materially derived from city contract revenue funding roughly 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget - a fact pattern closely tracking the imputed spousal-interest theory that produced liability in People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 and voidance-style reasoning in Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201.

The spousal-salary interest does not obviously fall within any enumerated 'remote interest' category under section 1091(b), so the disclose-and-abstain cure mechanism section 1091 provides for remote interests is likely unavailable for that piece of the conflict, leaving it as a direct, non-curable-by-abstention interest under core section 1090.

Even if Okafor's own uncompensated board seat could arguably be treated as a 'noninterest' under a section 1091.5(a)(8)-type provision for noncompensated nonprofit officers (plausibly supported by the 2019 resolution designating her as council liaison), that provision would not reach the analytically separate and larger spousal-employment interest, so resignation from the board alone plus recusal likely would not fully cure the problem while the spouse remains employed on city-funded dollars.

Enforcement realities favor a challenge actually surfacing here: a losing bidder (one of only two other realistic competitors) or a taxpayer under CCP section 526a has clear standing and strong incentive to challenge an award to a bidder with a 60% historical win rate and a visible spousal/board nexus, making judicial testing of the non-cure-by-recusal doctrine plausible rather than merely theoretical.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the specific quantitative and structural facts here (a fixed salaried position not contingent on this particular contract, with only ~40% of the employer's overall budget attributable to city funds) satisfy the materiality/nexus threshold courts actually require to find a disqualifying 'financial interest' under section 1090, and the exact current text/scope of the section 1091.5 noninterest provisions for nonprofit officers/directors, which could partially or wholly change the analysis if they are broader (or narrower) than I am recalling.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not the proposition is correct: absent Okafor's resignation from Harbor Light's board (and possibly a change in her spouse's compensation structure), recusal from the RFP and award votes alone probably does not cure a § 1090 problem, because (1) Okafor's own board seat is a personal governance-level interest that sits uneasily with the narrow § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer 'noninterest' exception, and (2) the spouse's salary, while nominally fixed, is tied closely enough to city-contract revenue (40% of Harbor Light's budget, 60% historical win rate) that it plausibly falls outside the 'compensation not dependent on the contract' limitation built into § 1091(b)'s remote-interest categories. Under Thomson v. Call and its progeny, a body member's disqualifying interest voids the contract regardless of recusal from the vote unless a statutory remote-interest (with disclosure+abstention) or noninterest exception cleanly applies — and on these compounding, repeat-awardee facts I think it more likely than not no clean exception saves the deal without resignation. That said, a genuine, non-frivolous counterargument exists that with (a) resignation from the board, (b) rigorous procedural recusal satisfying § 1091(a), and (c) confirmation the spouse's pay is a true fixed non-contingent employee salary, the city could lawfully proceed — but the proposition as framed keeps the board seat and the spousal job intact, which is the weaker fact pattern for lawfulness.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal from the vote, because § 1090's prohibition runs to the whole body, not just to the interested member's ballot.

Okafor's uncompensated Harbor Light board seat is arguably a personal, governance-level financial interest that is not obviously covered by the narrow nonprofit-officer 'noninterest' exception in § 1091.5(a), particularly because as a council-designated liaison/director she retains influence over the very entity receiving city funds.

The spouse's ostensibly 'fixed' $95k salary is plausibly not truly independent of this contract given that 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget derives from city contracts and Harbor Light has won ~60% of such awards historically, which cuts against the 'compensation not dependent on the contract' premise underlying § 1091(b) remote-interest treatment of spousal employees.

California courts construe § 1090 strictly and resolve close calls against public officials because the statute targets the temptation and appearance of self-dealing, not just actual corruption, making a clean judicial finding of a full statutory cure less likely on these aggravated, compounding facts.

A real counter-scenario exists — if the spouse is a genuine rank-and-file, non-officer employee whose pay is demonstrably unaffected by this specific award, and Okafor's board seat independently qualifies as a § 1091.5(a) noninterest, then disclosure plus rigorous multi-stage recusal (not just an abstained vote) could validly cure the conflict without resignation, meaning the proposition could be false — this keeps my estimate below high-confidence territory.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether Okafor's uncompensated Harbor Light board seat actually qualifies for a § 1091.5(a) 'noninterest' exception and whether the spouse's salary counts as a true § 1091(b) 'remote interest' (compensation not dependent on the contract) despite Harbor Light's 40% revenue dependency on city contracts and its 60% historical win rate — I could not confidently verify the exact statutory subsections/conditions, and the outcome turns entirely on whether these fact-sensitive exceptions apply cleanly to this compounded board-seat-plus-spousal-salary situation.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not TRUE: on these facts Okafor probably holds a disqualifying "financial interest" in the Harbor Light contract (via her community-property interest in her spouse's contract-dependent salary, and arguably via her own board seat/fiduciary role), no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception appears to reach a spouse's substantial, contract-tied salary from the bidding entity, and — critically — California's § 1090 doctrine holds that a sitting governing-body member's disqualifying interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal or abstention from the vote. The main soft spot is whether a court would treat the spousal salary (paid from a commingled general operating budget rather than a contract-specific fee) as sufficiently direct to be a § 1090 interest at all, versus treating it as purely a Political Reform Act materiality question where recusal would suffice.
rationales: California case law (Stigall v. City of Taft, Thomson v. Call, People v. Honig) establishes that a governing-body member's disqualifying financial interest is NOT cured by that member's recusal/abstention from the vote — the contract remains void if any member of the body is interested, because § 1090's purpose targets behind-the-scenes influence, not just tainted votes.

Okafor plausibly holds a disqualifying interest because her spouse's ~$95K salary is community property income (Fam. Code §§ 751, 760) and is materially dependent on the very contract at issue (Harbor Light draws ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts and the spouse runs the funded programs).

Neither § 1091's remote-interest categories nor § 1091.5's noninterest exceptions appear tailored to cover a spouse's substantial, contract-dependent salary from the bidding nonprofit itself — those provisions more plausibly reach only Okafor's own uncompensated board seat (verify subdivision, e.g., § 1091(b) or § 1091.5(a)(8)).

Okafor's concurrent fiduciary duty to Harbor Light as director, while she helps shape the RFP scoring criteria that entity will be judged under, is precisely the structural dual-loyalty harm § 1090's strict rule is designed to prevent, independent of the spousal-salary issue.

If an unexcepted § 1090 interest exists, the remedy under Gov. Code § 1092 and Thomson v. Call is that the contract is void (not merely voidable and not curable by estoppel), with attendant disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure, none of which is remedied by good-faith recusal alone.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court would actually classify the spouse's general-operating-budget salary as a sufficiently direct/substantial "financial interest ... in the contract" to trigger § 1090 itself (versus treating this as chiefly a Political Reform Act §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, for which recusal is the recognized cure) — this classification, plus the exact scope of the § 1091/1091.5 exceptions (subdivisions unverified), is the pivot point that could flip the answer.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not void or voidable under section 1090 despite full recusal. Okafor's spouse's salary at Harbor Light is a community-property financial interest tied to a revenue stream (city/HHAP contracts fund ~40% of Harbor Light's budget) that the RFP framework and award directly control, and settled section 1090 doctrine treats a non-exempt financial interest held by any member of the contracting body as tainting the body's power to make the contract, regardless of that member's individual abstention. The closest on-point analogy, People v. Honig, involved a spouse's compensation from an entity benefiting from publicly steered funding and produced a section 1090 conviction, which cuts strongly toward voidness here. The one soft spot is that Okafor's own uncompensated, resolution-designated board seat may independently qualify for a section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest exception, but that would only neutralize one of two independent disqualifying interests -- the spousal-salary interest remains uncured by disclosure-and-abstention procedures because no enumerated exception squarely reaches a spouse's paid employment with a contracting nonprofit.
rationales: People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 is closely analogous: a spouse's compensation from a nonprofit that benefited from publicly directed funding supported a section 1090 conviction, and that logic transfers directly to Okafor's spouse's Harbor Light salary.

Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, section 1090's prohibition attaches to the governing body's act of contracting whenever any member has a non-exempt financial interest, and the interested member's recusal or abstention from voting does not by itself cure the violation, unlike the Political Reform Act's recusal-based cure under section 87100 et seq.

No section 1091 remote-interest or section 1091.5 noninterest category squarely covers a spouse's non-nominal salaried employment with a nonprofit that is a contract counterparty; the categories that plausibly fit (noncompensated nonprofit officer/director provisions) address Okafor's own board seat, not the separate and arguably more serious spousal-salary interest.

Because roughly 40% of Harbor Light's operating budget -- the source of the spouse's ~$95,000 salary -- derives from city contracts, and the RFP directly determines whether that revenue stream continues, the interest is not so remote or speculative as to escape section 1090's broadly construed 'financially interested' standard.

Even if a court found Okafor's own board seat protected by a resolution-based section 1091/1091.5 exception, section 1090 voids the contract if any single officer involved with the contracting body has an uncured disqualifying interest, so the spousal interest alone is sufficient to taint the contract.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary (as opposed to compensation directly tied to the specific contract's proceeds, like commission or bonus) as too remote or speculative to constitute a disqualifying section 1090 interest, since no squarely on-point civil (non-criminal) appellate decision addresses this precise combination of a designated board seat plus a spouse's fixed nonprofit salary partially funded by the contracting agency.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not correct: recusal probably does NOT cure the § 1090 problem here, because the facts (spouse's salary funded ~40% by city contracts, Harbor Light's ~60% historical win rate) point toward a direct/prohibited "financial interest" imputed to Okafor through her spouse's employment, not a merely "remote interest" under § 1091 that disclosure-and-abstention could validate. Under Thomson v. Call, a prohibited § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body cannot be cured by that member's recusal from the vote — the taint runs to the whole body/contract, not just the individual's ballot. Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently qualify as a § 1091.5 "noninterest" (nonprofit-liaison exception), which would remove one prong of the problem, but that exception doesn't reach the separate and more serious spousal-salary interest. The main source of doubt is genuine ambiguity over whether the spousal employment interest is properly "remote" (fixed salary not tied to any one contract, arguably analogous to § 1091(b)(2)) rather than "direct," which would flip the analysis and let a well-executed recusal-plus-disclosure procedure save the contract.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a disqualifying § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body is not cured by that member's abstention from the vote, because the statutory prohibition runs to the whole body, not just the individual's ballot.

The spouse's ~$95,000 salary is community property (Fam. Code § 760) and California courts construe 'financially interested' under § 1090 broadly, resolving doubts in favor of disqualification (e.g., Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), supporting imputation of the spouse's interest to Okafor.

The facts emphasize that Harbor Light's general operating budget (which funds the spouse's salary) is ~40% city-contract-derived and that Harbor Light wins ~60% of these awards, a close enough nexus between this specific $2.3M RFP and the spouse's job/compensation to plausibly push the interest out of § 1091's 'remote interest' category and into a direct, non-curable interest.

Even if Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies as a § 1091.5 'noninterest' for noncompensated officers of an affiliated/liaison nonprofit, that exception is narrow and would not immunize the distinct spousal-employment financial interest, which is the more serious defect.

A credible counterargument exists that the spouse's fixed salary (not contingent on any single contract's award) could instead be framed as a § 1091(b)-type remote interest curable by disclosure-plus-abstention, which is the main reason the probability is not higher than roughly two-thirds.

rounds.position_summary: Still more likely than not TRUE, but I trim slightly from my round-1 estimate of 78 to 75. The structural rule that a member's recusal does not cure a § 1090 violation when she sits on the very contracting body (Thomson v. Call; Stigall) is solid and essentially uncontested across the panel, so if a disqualifying financial interest exists and no exception applies, the contract is void regardless of recusal. My residual uncertainty is genuinely conjunctive: (1) whether the spouse's fixed, general-fund-derived salary actually clears the § 1090 'financial interest' threshold at all (as opposed to being too indirect/non-contingent to count), and (2) if it does, whether any § 1091(b) remote-interest category or § 1091.5 noninterest exception could still save it. The panel summary did not resolve either question, but it sharpened my sense that both are independently live sources of doubt rather than one dominant uncertainty (as I framed it in round 1), which modestly lowers my confidence in the conjunction — even though I still think disqualification is the better-supported reading on these facts (senior, city-funded management position; ~40% budget dependence on city contracts; ~60% historical win rate on this type of RFP).
rationales: The Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 / Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 structural rule — that an interested member's recusal does not cure § 1090 for the body itself absent a fitting exception — was affirmed by all seven panelists and remains, in my view, the most solid doctrinal anchor here; it is what keeps the proposition well above 50 whenever a qualifying interest exists and no exception applies.

The panel's emphasis on Family Code §§ 751/760 community-property doctrine (raised by 5/7 panelists, which I had underweighted explicitly in round 1) supplies a clean legal mechanism for imputing the spouse's salary to Okafor as 'her own' financial interest rather than a merely derivative or sympathetic one — this strengthens, not weakens, the threshold 'interest exists' step of the analysis.

Disagreement point 3 in the panel summary — that some panelists locate the pivotal doubt at whether a fixed, non-contingent salary counts as a § 1090 'financial interest' at all, rather than assuming that step is settled and placing all doubt in exception-fit — persuades me that I was stacking two independent uncertain inferential steps into one in round 1; properly treated as conjunctive uncertainties, the overall probability should be modestly lower than 78, even though each step individually still favors the 'interest exists / no exception applies' reading.

Theme F (4/7 panelists affirmatively finding no enumerated § 1091/1091.5 category covers spousal employment with a contracting nonprofit) is reassuring but not dispositive, since Disagreement point 2 shows genuine, unresolved division among equally well-reasoned panelists on whether such a remote-interest category exists and could be invoked — I do not think this splits enough evidence to change my estimate much in either direction, since it mirrors the uncertainty I already flagged in round 1 as my 'biggest uncertainty.'

The quantitative facts (40% of Harbor Light's operating budget from city contracts; 60% historical win rate; Director-of-Programs seniority) continue to do the heaviest lifting for me in distinguishing this from the 'fixed salary from a large, diversified employer' fact pattern that AG opinions and remote-interest case law sometimes treat as too attenuated to disqualify — these facts push the spousal interest toward direct/material rather than remote, which is why I remain well above the panel's low end despite trimming my estimate.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the threshold question — is a spouse's fixed, non-contingent, general-operating-budget-funded salary even a § 1090 'financial interest' capable of being 'affected' by this specific contract — is itself genuinely contestable (as some panelists suggested), such that the analysis could resolve at step one (no interest, so no § 1090 problem, only a PRA § 87100/87103 recusal question) rather than proceeding to the exception-fit step where I placed most of my doubt in round 1. I now see this as two stacked uncertain inferences rather than one, which is the main driver of my modest downward revision.
rounds.response_to_panel: I moved from 78 to 75 — a small but deliberate adjustment — for one specific reason drawn from the panel's Disagreement point 3: several panelists located the pivotal doubt at the prior analytical step (does a fixed, non-contingent spousal salary even constitute a § 1090 'financial interest' at all) rather than, as I did in round 1, treating that step as essentially settled and putting all my uncertainty into exception-fit. Recognizing these as two independently uncertain, conjunctive steps (interest-exists AND no-exception-applies) rather than one dominant uncertainty is a valid structural correction to my reasoning, and it warrants a modest reduction in the overall probability even though I still find each step individually more likely to favor disqualification than not. I did not move further down toward the panel median/mean, because I don't find the 'wisdom of crowds' convergence itself a valid reason to shade my estimate absent an identifiable substantive argument, and most of the other themes (community-property imputation, the strength of the Thomson/Stigall structural rule, the quantitative revenue-dependence facts) if anything reinforce rather than undercut my view that disqualification is the better-supported reading. I also considered and rejected moving further based on Theme K/L (enforcement likelihood, fiduciary duty) since those go to practical consequences rather than the legal correctness of the proposition itself, which is what's being asked.

rounds.position_summary: Still more likely than not that the contract is void/voidable under § 1090 despite full recusal. The panel summary supplied legitimate reasons to move slightly above my round-1 estimate of 70, but I stop short of the upper cluster (78) because a genuine, unresolved doubt remains about whether a fixed, non-contract-contingent spousal salary clears the § 1090 "financial interest" threshold at all, versus being essentially a PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality question that recusal would fully cure.
rationales: The structural rule is essentially uncontested and does the heaviest lifting: under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, § 1090's prohibition attaches to the contracting body as a whole once any member has a disqualifying financial interest, so recusal from both the RFP-framework and award votes does not by itself free the council to contract with Harbor Light — 7/7 panelists agreed, matching my prior view, and this anchors my estimate well above 50.

Panel theme C (revenue-dependence nexus, 6/7) is a genuine addition I underweighted in round 1: Harbor Light's ~40% general-operating dependence on city contracts and ~60% historical win rate convert the spousal salary from a purely fixed/speculative interest into one with a demonstrable, quantifiable link to this specific award — this directly undercuts my round-1 'biggest uncertainty' and justifies moving up rather than merely flagging doubt.

Community-property grounding (Fam. Code §§ 751/760, panel theme B) strengthens the imputation step by giving Okafor her own statutory financial interest in the spouse's earnings independent of the board seat, and the seniority point (theme J: Director of Programs is management-level, not a low-level or non-supervisory employee) cuts against any argument that a rank-and-file-style remote-interest exception could apply — both push toward no available cure via § 1091/1091.5, reinforcing voidness.

I did not fully credit panel theme F's affirmative claim that 'no enumerated exception reaches spousal employment' because the panel itself flagged this as a genuine, unresolved split (Disagreement point 2): some remote-interest categories in § 1091(b) address officers/employees of the contracting party, and if courts extend that category (via imputation) to a spouse's employment, disclosure-and-abstention could validate the contract even without board resignation — I treat this as live uncertainty rather than resolved in favor of voidness.

I retain meaningful weight for the alternative framing in Disagreement point 3(a): that a fixed, non-commission salary unaffected contract-by-contract might not constitute a § 1090 'financial interest' at all, making this fundamentally a PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality analysis where recusal is the established, sufficient cure — this is the main reason I stay in the low-to-mid 70s rather than joining the 78 cluster.

rounds.position_summary: Still more likely than not TRUE; I am nudging up slightly from my round-1 figure of 72 to 75. The panel's near-unanimous structural point (7/7) that section 1090's non-cure-by-recusal rule applies to a sitting body member regardless of how clean the recusal is means the analysis largely collapses to one fork: does Okafor hold a disqualifying, non-excepted 'financial interest' in the Harbor Light contract at all (principally via community-property imputation of her spouse's ~$95K salary, ~40% city-funded)? Once that threshold is crossed, non-curability by recusal is close to automatic under California doctrine, and any board-seat noninterest exception (even if it applies) would not reach the analytically separate spousal-employment interest. The panel's tight convergence (67-78, std dev 4.6) plus several reinforcing points I independently find persuasive - the spouse's director-level/management role cutting against any 'rank-and-file employee' remote-interest argument, and section 1092's voidness remedy being a deliberate structural feature rather than an incidental one - moved me modestly upward. I did not move further because genuine, unresolved uncertainty remains about whether a fixed, non-contingent, general-operating-budget salary crosses the 'financial interest' threshold under section 1090 case law, and about whether any enumerated section 1091(b) remote-interest category could plausibly be stretched to cover spousal employment with a disclose-and-abstain cure - a live disagreement among informed panelists I cannot confidently resolve without verifying current statutory text.
rationales: Once a disqualifying financial interest is established for a sitting council member, California's rule that section 1090 disables the contracting body itself (not merely the interested member) makes recusal from the vote structurally insufficient to cure the defect - essentially undisputed doctrine among the panel and consistent with Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 - so the entire probability mass turns on whether an interest exists and is unexcepted, not on the quality of the recusal.

The spouse's title (Director of Programs, a management/policy-level position, not rank-and-file) reinforces that even if some remote-interest category exists for lower-level or non-decision-adjacent employees of a contracting nonprofit, it likely would not extend to this role, narrowing the panel-identified 'exception fit' escape hatch (disagreement point 2 in the summary) and supporting a higher rather than lower probability.

Government Code section 1092's voidness/disgorgement remedy structurally exists to police exactly this scenario - a technically clean recusal by an interested member while the body still acts - which reinforces that the statutory scheme is designed to prevent the very 'recusal is enough' outcome Okafor is hoping for, rather than being a technicality courts would overlook given the substantial 40%/60% revenue-dependence facts.

The spousal-salary interest still does not fit comfortably within any enumerated section 1091(b) remote-interest category I can confidently identify (spousal employment with the contracting party, as opposed to the official's own remote employment relationship, is not clearly enumerated), and a majority of panelists (4/7, skewing toward higher-probability panelists) affirmatively asserted no such exception reaches it - this is a reason I find independently valid and it modestly increases my confidence over round 1.

The residual and most legitimate source of doubt, acknowledged by all seven panelists in round 1, is whether a fixed salary paid from a diversified general operating budget (not contingent on winning this specific RFP) is 'direct' enough to constitute a section 1090 financial interest under current case law; this uncertainty is real and keeps me well short of near-certainty, but I do not think it overcomes the weight of Honig-style imputation reasoning and the specific 40%/60% facts pled in this hypothetical.

rounds.position_summary: Revising upward from 68 to 74: the proposition is more likely than not correct. Two structural pillars remain decisive: (1) under Thomson v. Call, a governing-body member's own disqualifying § 1090 interest voids the contract regardless of that member's recusal from the vote, because the prohibition runs to the body's act of contracting, not just to the interested member's ballot; and (2) on these compounding facts (spouse holds a Director-level, non-rank-and-file position; Harbor Light derives ~40% of its operating budget from city contracts; Harbor Light has won ~60% of prior awards), the 'fixed salary, independent of the contract' characterization needed to fit any § 1091(b) remote-interest cure is genuinely strained. What moved me from round 1 is a point I underweighted: Okafor's interest in her spouse's salary is plausibly not merely a Political Reform Act materiality question but a direct § 1090 'financially interested' predicate in its own right, grounded in community-property law (Fam. Code § 760) and, if accurately recalled, precedent applying that theory to void a public contract benefiting a spouse's employer (Fraser-Yamor Agency v. County of Del Norte, cite unverified). That reframes the analysis: even if Okafor's own board seat is separately cured via a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer noninterest, that cure does not reach the distinct, likely-uncured spousal-salary interest — and the proposition's facts keep both the board seat and the spousal job intact, changing nothing but recusal. The proposition's disjunctive 'void or voidable' framing is also a comparatively easy bar to clear. I stay below the top of the panel's range because the exact statutory text of § 1091(b)'s remote-interest categories and § 1091.5(a)'s conditions remains unverified by me, and a non-frivolous 'fixed, non-contingent salary is too attenuated to be an interest at all' counterargument survives every panelist's own scrutiny, including mine.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 establish that § 1090's prohibition attaches to the body's act of contracting whenever any member has a disqualifying interest, so an interested member's recusal from the vote does not itself cure the defect absent a clean statutory remote-interest or noninterest exception — this structural point, present in my round-1 answer, was reinforced rather than undercut by the panel's unanimous agreement (7/7).

Community-property law (Fam. Code § 760, and per some panelists Fam. Code § 751) plausibly supplies a direct theory that Okafor herself is 'financially interested' in the contract because her spouse's salary is community property in which Okafor has a present, equal interest — this is a stronger and more direct route to a § 1090 interest than treating spousal income as relevant only to the Political Reform Act's § 87103 materiality analysis, and if Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201 in fact applied this theory to void a contract benefiting a spouse's employer (citation and holding unverified), that is squarely on point.

Separating the two interests analytically (as several panelists did) clarifies the outcome: even assuming Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies for a § 1091.5(a) nonprofit-officer/director noninterest, that exception is personal to her governance role and does not extend to cure the distinct financial interest created by her spouse's compensated employment with the same contracting nonprofit — and the proposition's facts do not include any change to that employment, only recusal.

The spouse's role as Director of Programs (a management/policy-adjacent position) makes the 'fixed salary wholly independent of the contract' characterization needed for a § 1091(b) remote-interest cure harder to sustain than it would be for a rank-and-file employee, especially layered onto the 40%-of-budget/60%-win-rate revenue-dependence facts, which push the interest toward 'direct and material' rather than remote or speculative under the strict-construction rule of Stigall and People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289.

The proposition is phrased disjunctively ('void or voidable'), which is an easier bar to satisfy than a pure voidness claim; combined with § 1092's remedial structure and § 1097's criminal exposure for willful violations (both of which exist precisely because the Legislature intended prophylactic, not merely compensatory, consequences), this modestly raises the probability that some legally cognizable defect attaches to the contract as structured, even if courts might differ on the void-versus-voidable characterization.

Honig, 48 Cal.App.4th 289, despite its distinguishable criminal/engineered-scheme facts) treats a spouse's employment with a contracting party as a genuine § 1090-type interest requiring exception analysis, not a lesser PRA-only concern curable by recusal.

summaries:
# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 72.0, mean 73.0, std dev 4.6, range 67.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 68.0 and 78.0.

*(For the skew notes below, "lower-probability" = the three panelists at 67–70; "higher-probability" = the four panelists at 72–78.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**A. Recusal cannot cure a body member's § 1090 interest (Thomson v. Call / Stigall structural rule) — 7 of 7.** Every panelist invoked the rule that § 1090's prohibition runs to the contracting body as a whole, so an interested member's abstention/recusal from the vote does not itself validate the contract. No skew.

**B. Spouse's salary as an imputable disqualifying financial interest — 7 of 7.** All panelists treated the spouse's ~$95K salary as the central candidate interest. Within this theme, **5 panelists** (2 lower, 3 higher) explicitly grounded imputation in community-property law (Fam. Code §§ 751/760).

**C. Revenue-dependence nexus (40% city-contract funding / 60% historical win rate) — 6 of 7.** Six panelists (2 lower, 4 higher) cited these quantitative facts as pushing the spousal interest toward "direct/material" rather than remote or speculative. One lower-probability panelist did not rely on the percentages.

**D. Board seat likely separately excepted, but the exception does not reach the spousal salary — 6 of 7.** Six panelists reasoned that Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may fall within a statutory exception (most citing a § 1091.5 noninterest; one citing § 1091 remote-interest treatment), while emphasizing that any such exception would not cover the distinct spousal-employment interest. One lower-probability panelist took the opposite view on the board seat (see Disagreements, point 1).

**E. Acknowledged counterargument: fixed, non-contingent salary may be remote or too attenuated — 7 of 7.** Every panelist identified some version of this as the principal reason their probability was not higher. Panelists differ on where this doubt sits analytically (see Disagreements, points 2–3).

**F. No enumerated § 1091/1091.5 category squarely covers spousal employment with the contracting entity — 4 of 7** (1 lower, 3 higher; slight skew toward higher-probability panelists). These panelists asserted affirmatively that the exception catalog does not reach the spousal salary, rather than treating exception-fit as an open question.

**G. Strict/broad construction of "financially interested," resolving doubts toward disqualification — 4 of 7** (2 lower, 2 higher). Citing Stigall, Honig, and the statute's prophylactic purpose.

**H. Contrast with the Political Reform Act (recusal cures § 87100 but not § 1090) — 3 of 7 as a rationale** (all at or above the median; two additional panelists cited PRA provisions in authorities "for contrast" only). Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**I. Void/structural remedy under § 1092 (with disgorgement and § 1097 criminal exposure) as reinforcing that recusal is insufficient by design — 3 of 7**, all at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**J. Seniority of the spouse's role (Director of Programs, management/policy-adjacent) as analytically significant — 2 of 7**, both at 78. Skews toward higher-probability panelists.

**K. Practical enforcement likelihood (losing-bidder or taxpayer standing under CCP § 526a) — 1 of 7.**

**L. Okafor's fiduciary duty to Harbor Light / dual-loyalty structural harm while shaping RFP criteria — 1 of 7.**

**M. Cure scenarios involving board resignation — 2 of 7**, reaching different conclusions (see Disagreements, point 4).

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Statutory classification of Okafor's own board seat.** Three incompatible readings appear: (a) most panelists treat the uncompensated board seat as a likely § 1091.5 "noninterest"; (b) one panelist classifies it as a § 1091 remote interest curable by disclosure-and-abstention; (c) one panelist argues it sits uneasily with the narrow § 1091.5(a) exception because it is a personal, governance-level interest carrying influence over the funded entity.

2. **Whether any § 1091(b) remote-interest category could reach the spousal salary at all.** Some panelists treat a spousal-employment/fixed-salary remote-interest route as a genuinely available path that could flip the outcome if properly invoked; others flatly state that no enumerated remote-interest or noninterest category covers a spouse's paid employment with the contracting nonprofit. These are incompatible readings of the exception catalog, not just different emphasis.

3. **Locus of the threshold doubt.** Panelists split on where the pivotal uncertainty lies: (a) whether the fixed, general-operating-budget salary is a § 1090 "financial interest" at all (with one panelist framing the alternative as a pure PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality question, where recusal would suffice), versus (b) assuming the interest exists and locating the uncertainty entirely in exception fit. These framings assign the same doubt to different analytical steps with different doctrinal consequences.

4. **What cure package (if any) short of the spouse leaving employment could work.** One panelist posits that board resignation + rigorous multi-stage recusal + confirmation of a genuine rank-and-file, non-contingent salary could make the contract lawful; another states that board resignation plus recusal likely would not cure the problem while the spouse remains employed on city-funded dollars.

5. **Whether the spouse's job title/seniority matters to exception fit.** Two panelists condition exception availability on the spouse being rank-and-file rather than a director-level employee; others analyze only the fixed-vs-contingent structure of compensation without treating role seniority as relevant.

# Persistent Disagreement Report — Final Round

**Overall posture:** The panel converged directionally (all seven ≥ 71; median/mean 75.0; std dev 2.3). No panelist disputes the proposition's direction. The residual 8-point spread is driven not by disagreement over the conclusion but by disagreement over *where the surviving doubt lives* and *how much it is worth*. Three distinct fault lines persist.

---

## Fault Line 1: The threshold-classification question (§ 1090 "financial interest" vs. PRA-only)

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- **Lower cluster (71, and the two 74s):** A spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary paid from a general operating budget only ~40% city-funded may fail to clear the § 1090 "financial interest … in the contract" threshold *at all*. If so, the matter is governed only by PRA §§ 87100/87103 materiality analysis — where recusal is the recognized and sufficient cure — and § 1090's no-cure-by-recusal rule never engages. The panelist at 71 explicitly notes the binary void/voidable framing does not accommodate this outcome, and one 74 states this threshold question is "where the real probability mass of 'proposition false' lives."
- **Upper cluster (77, 79):** The threshold question resolves *in favor of* finding an interest. The 77 panelist reports that spousal employment with a contracting party is treated in California municipal-conflict practice (ILG/AG guidance, Honig-adjacent reasoning) as a cognizable § 1090 interest independent of PRA analysis; the 79 panelist notes no panelist surfaced authority treating a fixed general-operating-budget salary as categorically outside § 1090.

**(b) Nature of the split:** A **genuinely unsettled legal question** — panelists on both sides characterize it as an unresolved doctrinal fork, and no one produced a verified, squarely on-point civil authority classifying this fact pattern. It is compounded by a **factual-characterization dispute over the same undisputed facts**: the 40% budget dependence / 60% win rate / director-level seniority are read by some (both 74s, 75s, 77, 79) as converting the interest into "direct and material," and by the 71 panelist as consistent with attenuation below the threshold.

**(c) What would resolve it:** A California appellate decision or AG opinion directly classifying a spouse's fixed, non-contingent salary at a nonprofit contractor as (or as not) a § 1090 interest; or new facts tying the spouse's position or compensation more directly to this specific contract (e.g., the position is contract-funded, or would be eliminated if the award were lost), which multiple panelists indicated would collapse the attenuation argument.

---

## Fault Line 2: The § 1091(b) exception-catalog question

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- One 74 panelist keeps live the possibility that a § 1091(b) remote-interest category addressing employees of a contracting party could be **extended via imputation to spousal employment**, making the defect curable by disclosure-and-abstention without resignation.
- The 75s, 77, and 79 treat the catalog as containing no fitting category; the 79 panelist adds that under strict-construction doctrine (Stigall/Honig), unresolved ambiguity about exception fit should be resolved *toward* disqualification — a canon-allocation argument no other panelist adopted or rebutted.
- The 71 panelist accepted the "no fitting category" premise but redirected its significance: absence of an exception pushes the doubt back to Fault Line 1 rather than eliminating it.

**(b) Nature of the split:** Partly a **genuinely unsettled legal question** (scope of the enumerated categories as applied to spouses), but substantially an **evidentiary/verification gap**: multiple panelists (both 74s, 75, 77) expressly state they cannot verify the current statutory text of § 1091(b) and § 1091.5(a), or the actual holding and citation of *Fraser-Yamor Agency v. County of Del Norte*, from memory. One panelist flagged that "a meaningful part of my upward revision loses its footing" if Fraser-Yamor does not hold what the panel recalled.

**(c) What would resolve it:** Verification of the current statutory text of §§ 1091(b) and 1091.5(a); confirmation of Fraser-Yamor's actual holding; or an authority addressing whether any remote-interest category reaches spousal (as opposed to the official's own) employment with a contracting party.

---

## Fault Line 3: How to structure and price the residual uncertainty

**(a) Crux each side rests on**

- One 75 panelist *reduced* their estimate specifically because they now see two **conjunctive** uncertain steps (interest-exists AND no-exception-applies) rather than one dominant uncertainty.
- The 79 panelist held near the top of the range on the ground that this same doubt was already priced into their round-1 band and that convergence supplied corroboration, not correction.
- The 71 panelist declined to move toward the median on the stated ground that Delphi convergence "reflects shared professional priors more than a resolved authority" and that majority head-count on a contested doctrinal classification is not itself evidence.

**(b) Nature of the split:** This fault line is **not doctrinal** — it reflects **differing epistemic/risk postures**: how to aggregate stacked uncertainties, and how much evidential weight (if any) to give panel convergence itself. Panelists reached different numbers from substantially overlapping doctrinal views.

**(c) What would resolve it:** Nothing external; this is a calibration-philosophy difference. It would dissolve only if Fault Lines 1 and 2 were resolved by authority, at which point the conjunction collapses.

---

## Points of no persistent disagreement (for completeness)

- The Thomson v. Call / Stigall structural rule — recusal does not cure a sitting member's disqualifying interest — was affirmed by all seven and is treated by every panelist as the load-bearing premise.
- Community-property imputation (Fam. Code §§ 751/760) as the mechanism making the spousal salary Okafor's *own* interest was accepted panel-wide.
- The separateness of the board-seat and spousal-salary interests — and that a § 1091.5-type cure of the former would not reach the latter — drew no final-round dissent.
- One panelist noted Disagreement 4 from earlier rounds (resignation as cure) is moot under the proposition's framing, which holds board seat and employment constant; no panelist contested that.

**Bottom line:** The persistent disagreement is narrow but precise: whether an attenuated spousal-salary interest is analyzed as a *threshold* § 1090 question (potentially exiting the statute entirely into PRA territory) or as an *exception-fit* question within § 1090 — a genuinely unsettled classification that no panelist could resolve with verified authority — overlaid with an unverified-sources problem and divergent calibration philosophies. The panel's spread maps almost exactly onto how much weight each panelist assigns the threshold-classification escape route.
